enrichment.short_description: MODX is a free, open-source PHP content management framework that gives developers full control over markup with no forced templates.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (open source); MODX Cloud hosting is custom-priced

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: MODX is an open-source PHP content management framework that began in 2004 as a fork of the Etomite CMS, and later grew into MODX Revolution. Unlike CMSs that ship with fixed themes, MODX has no built-in front-end markup — developers build the HTML output themselves using Templates, Chunks, and Snippets, giving full control over the final page structure.

The core software is free to download and self-host on any PHP/MySQL server. MODX LLC, the company behind the project, also runs MODX Cloud, a separate managed hosting service for MODX-built sites with staging, a CDN, and a web application firewall.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: MODX suits web agencies and developers who want to build fully custom marketing sites, portals, or multi-site setups without fighting a CMS's default theme system or plugin conventions.

features.title: Template, Chunk & Snippet architecture
features.description: Build page output from reusable Templates, Chunks, and PHP Snippets instead of a fixed theme engine.

features.title: Access Control Lists
features.description: Granular user-group and permission policies control who can edit which resources and elements.

features.title: Contexts for multi-site management
features.description: Run multiple related sites or sections from a single MODX install using Contexts.

features.title: Friendly URLs
features.description: Clean, SEO-friendly URL structures configurable per resource.

features.title: MODX Extras repository
features.description: Community-built add-ons for e-commerce, forms, SEO, and more, installable via the package manager.

features.title: Resource revision history
features.description: Track and roll back changes to content resources.

features.title: MODX Cloud hosting
features.description: Optional managed hosting with selective staging, a CDN/WAF, and migration tune-ups for MODX sites.

pros: No forced theme or markup — complete design freedom
Free, open-source core with no licensing fees
Fine-grained access control for multi-editor teams
Long-running project (since 2004) with an established community
Native multi-site support via Contexts
cons: Steeper learning curve than turnkey CMSs like WordPress
Smaller extras/plugin ecosystem than mainstream competitors
Requires PHP/MySQL server knowledge to self-host
MODX Cloud pricing isn't published and requires contacting sales

faqs.question: Is MODX free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. The MODX CMS/CMF software is free and open source. MODX Cloud managed hosting is a separate, paid service.

faqs.question: What does MODX run on?
faqs.answer: MODX is a PHP content management framework that runs on a standard PHP/MySQL (or MariaDB) server stack.

faqs.question: Does MODX come with built-in themes?
faqs.answer: No. MODX has no default front-end theme; developers create the HTML output using Templates, Chunks, and Snippets.

faqs.question: Can I self-host MODX?
faqs.answer: Yes, MODX can be installed on any compatible PHP/MySQL server, or run on the official MODX Cloud platform.

use_cases.title: Fully custom marketing websites
use_cases.description: Agencies build bespoke sites with exact control over markup, without theme constraints.

use_cases.title: Multi-site management
use_cases.description: Organizations run several related websites from one MODX install using Contexts.
use_cases.audience: Businesses managing multiple brands or microsites

enrichment.seo_meta_description: MODX is a free, open-source PHP CMS/CMF with no forced templates, full access control, and multi-site support, plus optional MODX Cloud hosting.

enrichment.short_description: Neos is a free, open-source PHP content management system with inline WYSIWYG editing and multi-site, multi-language publishing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Neos is an open-source content management system built on the Neos Flow PHP framework. It grew out of the TYPO3 Phoenix project, was renamed TYPO3 Neos in 2012, and became an independent project (Neos) after splitting from the TYPO3 Association in 2015. It's licensed under GPL v3 or later and maintained by the Neos Foundation and community contributors.
Neos combines true inline WYSIWYG content editing with an application-development layer, so developers can extend it with custom Flow packages rather than being limited to a plugin marketplace.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Neos targets organizations and agencies that need multi-site, multi-language content management with real editorial workflows, and PHP developers comfortable working with the Flow framework.

features.title: Inline WYSIWYG editing
features.description: Edit content directly on the live page layout rather than in a separate backend form.

features.title: Multi-site and multi-language support
features.description: Manage several sites and languages from a single Neos installation.

features.title: Content dimensions
features.description: Vary content by language, region, or audience segment for targeting and personalization.

features.title: Workspace-based publishing
features.description: Draft and review content in isolated workspaces before publishing to the live site.

features.title: Role-based access control
features.description: Assign editors and administrators permissions scoped to specific content areas.

features.title: Flow framework extensibility
features.description: Extend Neos with custom packages built on the underlying Neos Flow PHP framework.

features.title: Media center
features.description: Central asset library for managing images and files across sites.

pros: Free and open source under GPL v3
True inline editing gives editors a live preview of changes
Strong multi-site and multi-language capabilities
Backed by a dedicated Neos Foundation and active contributor community
cons: Smaller plugin/theme ecosystem than WordPress or Drupal
Requires familiarity with the Flow PHP framework, adding a learning curve
Fewer ready-made hosting or support vendors than more mainstream CMSs

faqs.question: Is Neos free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Neos CMS is released under the GPL v3 (or later) license and is free to download and self-host.

faqs.question: What is Neos built on?
faqs.answer: Neos is built on the Neos Flow PHP framework, which provides both content management and application-development capabilities.

faqs.question: Who maintains Neos?
faqs.answer: Neos is maintained by the Neos Foundation together with open-source community contributors.

faqs.question: Can Neos manage multiple languages and sites?
faqs.answer: Yes, Neos has native multi-site and multi-language support through its content dimensions feature.

use_cases.title: Multi-language enterprise sites
use_cases.description: Organizations publish content across multiple languages and regions from one install.

use_cases.title: Custom content-driven applications
use_cases.description: Developers combine content management with custom application logic via the Flow framework.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Neos is a free, open-source PHP CMS with inline WYSIWYG editing, multi-site and multi-language support, built on the Neos Flow framework.

enrichment.short_description: Pimcore is an open-source platform for content management, product information management (PIM), and digital asset management (DAM).

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Pimcore is an open-core data and experience management platform combining a CMS, product information management (PIM), master data management (MDM), and digital asset management (DAM) in one system. It's developed by Pimcore GmbH, headquartered in Salzburg, Austria.

The Community Edition is free and open source, built on the same core as the commercial editions. Pimcore also sells Professional, Enterprise, and PaaS licenses that add commercial support, additional modules such as an e-commerce framework and customer data platform (CDP), and managed hosting.

Pimcore has been expanding its AI capabilities under the Pimcore Copilot and Agent SDK initiatives, adding AI-assisted actions, workflow automation, and integrations with external AI/ML providers across its data-management products.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Pimcore fits mid-size to large organizations that need to manage product data, digital assets, and content together — retailers, manufacturers, and enterprises running omnichannel commerce.

features.title: Unified data platform
features.description: Combines CMS, PIM, MDM, and DAM so product and content data live in one system.

features.title: Open-core Community Edition
features.description: Free, self-hostable core covering the fundamental content and data management capabilities.

features.title: Pimcore Copilot
features.description: AI-driven productivity tooling with customizable, context-aware actions and a job execution engine.

features.title: No seat or asset limits
features.description: Licensing isn't tied to user counts or the number of products/assets managed.

features.title: Pimcore Studio
features.description: A modern, React-based admin interface introduced to replace the legacy ExtJS UI.

features.title: E-commerce framework and CDP
features.description: Enterprise Edition adds an e-commerce framework and customer data platform module.

features.title: Managed PaaS hosting
features.description: Fully managed, cloud-hosted deployment option with 24/7 support and monitoring.

pros: Free, open-source Community Edition with the same core as paid tiers
Combines CMS, PIM, MDM, and DAM instead of requiring separate tools
Flat licensing that doesn't scale with product/asset volume
Active development, including recent AI-assisted features
cons: Commercial editions are expensive for smaller teams ($9,900+/year)
Full feature set (e-commerce, CDP, AI extras) requires paid Enterprise or PaaS tiers
Complex platform with a learning curve for teams new to PIM/DAM systems

faqs.question: Is Pimcore free?
faqs.answer: The Community Edition is free and open source. Professional, Enterprise, and PaaS editions are paid commercial licenses with additional modules and support.

faqs.question: What does Pimcore Professional cost?
faqs.answer: According to Pimcore's own pricing page, the Professional Edition starts at $9,900 per year for on-premises deployment.

faqs.question: Where is Pimcore based?
faqs.answer: Pimcore GmbH is headquartered in Salzburg, Austria.

faqs.question: Does Pimcore have AI features?
faqs.answer: Yes, Pimcore has introduced AI-assisted tooling including Pimcore Copilot and an Agent SDK for automating data and content management tasks.

use_cases.title: Product information management
use_cases.description: Centralize and syndicate product data across e-commerce, marketplaces, and print.

use_cases.title: Digital asset management
use_cases.description: Store, tag, and distribute media assets across marketing channels.

use_cases.title: Enterprise content management
use_cases.description: Manage websites and content alongside product and customer data.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Pimcore is an open-source platform unifying CMS, PIM, MDM, and DAM, with a free Community Edition and paid Professional/Enterprise/PaaS licenses.

enrichment.short_description: Plone is a free, open-source enterprise content management system built on Python, known for its long security track record.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Plone is an open-source content management system that has been developed since 2001, built on the Python-based Zope application server with a React front end for its editing interface. It's backed by the nonprofit Plone Foundation and a community of contributors.
Plone is 100% free with no licensing fees, and offers a REST API for headless deployments alongside its traditional server-rendered site management.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Plone is commonly used by government agencies, universities, and organizations with strict security and compliance requirements that value a long-standing, actively maintained open-source CMS.

features.title: Python/Zope architecture
features.description: Built on the Zope application server with a modern React-based editing interface.

features.title: REST API
features.description: Native REST API supports headless and decoupled front-end architectures.

features.title: Granular permissions and workflow
features.description: Fine-grained content workflow and role-based permissions suited to large organizations.

features.title: Long-term security focus
features.description: A security track record the project has maintained for over two decades.

features.title: On-premises or cloud deployment
features.description: Can be self-hosted on-premises or deployed in the cloud.

features.title: Extensible add-on architecture
features.description: Customize and extend functionality through Plone add-ons.

pros: Completely free and open source, forever, per the Plone Foundation
Strong, long-documented security history
Native REST API for headless use cases
Mature workflow and permissions system suited to complex organizations
Backed by a nonprofit foundation rather than a single vendor
cons: Python/Zope stack is less common than PHP-based CMSs, narrowing the hosting/developer pool
Smaller theme and add-on ecosystem than WordPress
Steeper setup and hosting requirements than typical shared-hosting CMSs

faqs.question: Is Plone free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Plone is 100% open source and free, with no licensing costs, per the Plone Foundation.

faqs.question: What is Plone built with?
faqs.answer: Plone runs on the Python-based Zope application server, with a React-based editing UI and a REST API.

faqs.question: How old is Plone?
faqs.answer: Plone was established in 2001 and has been continuously developed since.

faqs.question: Can Plone be used as a headless CMS?
faqs.answer: Yes, Plone provides a REST API that supports headless and decoupled front-end architectures.

use_cases.title: Government and institutional websites
use_cases.description: Organizations with strict security/compliance needs run Plone for public-facing and intranet sites.

use_cases.title: Headless content delivery
use_cases.description: Teams use Plone's REST API to power custom front ends while managing content centrally.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Plone is a free, open-source Python CMS with a REST API, granular workflow permissions, and a long-documented security track record since 2001.

enrichment.short_description: Publify is a free, open-source Ruby on Rails blogging platform, formerly known as Typo, in active development since 2004.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Publify (formerly Typo) is an open-source, multi-user blogging engine built on Ruby on Rails, in development since 2004. It bills itself as the oldest Ruby on Rails open-source project still maintained, and remains active on GitHub.

Publify covers self-hosted blogging with built-in text-formatting options (Markdown, Textile), a plugin/widget system, and themeable templates, in keeping with IndieWeb-style self-hosting and content ownership.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Publify suits Ruby developers and self-hosting enthusiasts who want a lightweight, self-owned blogging platform rather than a hosted service.

features.title: Multi-user blogging
features.description: Supports multiple authors publishing to the same blog.

features.title: Text-formatting options
features.description: Built-in support for Markdown, Textile, and other filtering languages for post content.

features.title: Theming system
features.description: Customizable themes for controlling blog appearance.

features.title: Widgets and plugins
features.description: Extend functionality through a widget and plugin system.

features.title: Social sharing integration
features.description: Includes short-messaging features connected to services like Twitter/X.

pros: Free and open source, self-hosted
One of the longest-running Ruby on Rails open-source projects
Multi-author support out of the box
Actively maintained on GitHub
cons: Much smaller community and plugin ecosystem than WordPress
Ruby on Rails hosting requirements are less beginner-friendly than PHP CMSs
Limited modern documentation compared to mainstream blogging platforms

faqs.question: Is Publify the same as Typo?
faqs.answer: Yes, Publify was originally released as Typo and was later renamed Publify.

faqs.question: Is Publify free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Publify is free, open-source software available on GitHub.

faqs.question: What is Publify built with?
faqs.answer: Publify is a Ruby on Rails application, requiring a Rails-compatible hosting environment.

faqs.question: Is Publify still maintained?
faqs.answer: Yes, its GitHub repository shows ongoing activity and it isn't archived.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted personal or team blogging
use_cases.description: Individuals or small teams run their own blog without relying on a hosted platform.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Publify (formerly Typo) is a free, open-source Ruby on Rails blogging platform with multi-author support, in development since 2004.

enrichment.short_description: REDAXO is a free, open-source PHP content management system originating in Germany, built for sites ranging from small to complex portals.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: REDAXO is an open-source PHP and MySQL content management system that has been developed since 2003, with a strong community base in Germany (the FriendsOfREDAXO community). It's free to download and self-host.

The system is structured around category-based content organization, block-based content input modules, and a media pool for managing files and images, with functionality extended through community-built AddOns.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: REDAXO is popular with German-speaking web developers and agencies building anything from small brochure sites to larger content portals.

features.title: Category-based structure
features.description: Organize site content and navigation through a category tree.

features.title: Block-based content input
features.description: Modular content blocks let editors assemble pages from reusable pieces.

features.title: Media pool
features.description: Central library for managing images and files across the site.

features.title: AddOn extensibility
features.description: Extend core functionality with community-built AddOns.

features.title: Open GitHub development
features.description: Development happens in the open, with community contributions welcomed.

pros: Free and open source, PHP/MySQL based
Active community (FriendsOfREDAXO) contributing AddOns and support
Flexible content-block system for varied page layouts
Established project with continuous development since 2003
cons: Documentation and community are primarily German-language
Smaller international adoption and ecosystem than PHP CMSs like WordPress
Requires PHP/MySQL server knowledge to self-host

faqs.question: Is REDAXO free?
faqs.answer: Yes, REDAXO is free and open-source software.

faqs.question: What language is REDAXO's community in?
faqs.answer: REDAXO's primary community and much of its documentation are in German, though the software itself is used internationally.

faqs.question: What does REDAXO run on?
faqs.answer: REDAXO is a PHP content management system that requires a MySQL-compatible database.

faqs.question: How long has REDAXO been developed?
faqs.answer: REDAXO has been in development since 2003.

use_cases.title: Small business websites
use_cases.description: Agencies build lightweight brochure and marketing sites.

use_cases.title: Complex content portals
use_cases.description: Larger organizations manage structured, category-driven content portals.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: REDAXO is a free, open-source PHP/MySQL CMS from Germany, in development since 2003, with category-based structure and community AddOns.

enrichment.short_description: SPIP is a free, open-source French publishing system focused on collaborative editing, multilingual sites, and ease of use.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: SPIP is a free, open-source web publishing system created in 2001 by minirézo, a French collective advocating for independent web presence and freedom of expression online. It's distributed under the GNU/GPL license.

SPIP emphasizes collaborative publishing, multilingual support, and simplicity, and supports translation into numerous languages beyond its French origin, including English, Spanish, German, Arabic, Russian, and Japanese.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: SPIP is used by nonprofit, institutional, personal, and commercial publishers, particularly in French-speaking regions, who need a collaborative editorial workflow.

features.title: Collaborative publishing workflow
features.description: Built around multi-editor, collective content operation.

features.title: Multilingual support
features.description: Interface and content translated into many languages, including French, English, Spanish, German, and more.

features.title: GNU/GPL licensing
features.description: Free to use for nonprofit, institutional, personal, or commercial sites.

features.title: Template language
features.description: SPIP's own templating syntax controls page rendering.

pros: Completely free and open source under GPL
Strong multilingual support out of the box
Designed from the ground up for collaborative/collective editing
Long track record since 2001
cons: Primary community and much documentation is French-language
Smaller international ecosystem and plugin base than mainstream CMSs
SPIP's custom template syntax adds a learning curve for developers used to other CMSs

faqs.question: Is SPIP free?
faqs.answer: Yes, SPIP is free, open-source software distributed under the GNU/GPL license.

faqs.question: Who created SPIP?
faqs.answer: SPIP was created in 2001 by minirézo, a French collective focused on independent web publishing.

faqs.question: Is SPIP only available in French?
faqs.answer: No, while SPIP originated in France, it supports many languages including English, Spanish, German, Arabic, and Russian.

faqs.question: What is SPIP good for?
faqs.answer: SPIP is designed for collaborative, multi-editor publishing across nonprofit, institutional, personal, and commercial sites.

use_cases.title: Collaborative editorial sites
use_cases.description: Teams of editors jointly manage and publish content.

use_cases.title: Multilingual publishing
use_cases.description: Organizations publish the same content across several languages.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: SPIP is a free, open-source French publishing system built for collaborative editing and multilingual websites, created in 2001.

enrichment.short_description: Squidex is an open-source, API-first headless CMS built on .NET, available self-hosted or as a managed cloud service.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Squidex is an open-source, API-first headless CMS created by Sebastian Stehle and released under the MIT license. It's built on .NET Core and lets teams manage structured content and deliver it to websites, apps, and server applications via REST and GraphQL APIs.

The software can be self-hosted for free via Docker or native binaries, or run through Squidex's managed cloud offering (cloud.squidex.io) for teams that don't want to operate their own infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Squidex fits development teams building multi-channel content experiences — websites, mobile apps, and other consumers of a content API — who want a self-hostable alternative to commercial headless CMS platforms.

features.title: REST and GraphQL APIs
features.description: Content is delivered through modern, developer-friendly API endpoints with OpenAPI support.

features.title: Content versioning
features.description: Full version history with rollback and audit logging.

features.title: Configurable workflows
features.description: Customizable approval and publishing workflows for content teams.

features.title: Event-driven architecture
features.description: Automate integrations with external systems via events.

features.title: Kubernetes-ready deployment
features.description: Ships with Helm charts for containerized, self-hosted deployments.

features.title: Open-source self-hosting
features.description: Free, MIT-licensed source available on GitHub with Docker-based installation.

pros: Free, MIT-licensed self-hosted option with no licensing costs
Modern API-first design with both REST and GraphQL
Built-in content versioning and audit logs
Kubernetes/Helm support for scalable self-hosted deployments
cons: Cloud plan pricing isn't published on the homepage
.NET-based stack may be less familiar to teams standardized on Node/PHP hosting
Smaller ecosystem than larger headless CMS competitors like Contentful or Strapi

faqs.question: Is Squidex free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the self-hosted version is free and open source under the MIT license. Squidex also offers a paid managed cloud service.

faqs.question: What is Squidex built with?
faqs.answer: Squidex is built on .NET Core and exposes content through REST and GraphQL APIs.

faqs.question: Can Squidex be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, Squidex can be deployed via Docker, native binaries, or Kubernetes/Helm charts on your own infrastructure.

faqs.question: Who created Squidex?
faqs.answer: Squidex was created by Sebastian Stehle.

use_cases.title: Headless content delivery
use_cases.description: Teams manage structured content and serve it to websites and apps via API.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted content infrastructure
use_cases.description: Organizations that need to keep content infrastructure in-house deploy Squidex via Docker or Kubernetes.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Squidex is an open-source, API-first headless CMS built on .NET, free to self-host under MIT license or run on Squidex Cloud.

enrichment.short_description: Superdesk is an open-source, headless newsroom management system for creating, curating, and publishing news content.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (open source); managed Superdesk Lite from €250/month

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Superdesk is an open-source, headless content management system purpose-built for newsrooms, developed by Sourcefabric, a nonprofit media technology organization headquartered in Prague, Czech Republic. Sourcefabric itself was spun off in 2010 from the Media Development Investment Fund's Campware project.

Superdesk provides end-to-end tools for news creation, curation, and distribution: content and document management, configurable editorial workflows, multi-source story building (wire services, RSS, social media), and publishing to web, print, mobile, and social channels.
The open-source version is free to self-host. Sourcefabric also offers Superdesk Lite, a managed package for smaller newsrooms of up to 25 users, starting at €250/month.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Superdesk is built for news agencies and publishers, from small newsrooms to large wire services, that need editorial workflow and multi-channel publishing tools.

features.title: Multi-source story building
features.description: Build stories from wire services, RSS, social media, and other inbound sources.

features.title: Editorial workflow management
features.description: Configurable workflows, collaboration tools, and access controls for newsroom teams.

features.title: Multi-channel publishing
features.description: Publish content to web, print, mobile, and social media from one system.

features.title: Content and document management
features.description: Metadata management, document classification, and version control for stories and assets.

features.title: Headless architecture
features.description: Web-based, headless CMS design lets newsrooms decouple content management from delivery.

features.title: Full-text search and filtering
features.description: Search and filter across the content library.

pros: Free, open-source core available to any newsroom
Purpose-built editorial workflow for journalism, not a generic CMS
Scales from small newsrooms to large wire services
Managed Superdesk Lite option for teams that don't want to self-host
cons: Managed hosting (Superdesk Lite) is a paid, subscription add-on
Niche, newsroom-specific tool not suited to general website content management
Self-hosting requires operational capacity for a specialized headless CMS

faqs.question: Is Superdesk free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the open-source version of Superdesk is free to download and self-host. Sourcefabric also sells managed Superdesk Lite packages starting at €250/month.

faqs.question: Who develops Superdesk?
faqs.answer: Superdesk is developed by Sourcefabric, a nonprofit media technology organization headquartered in Prague, Czech Republic.

faqs.question: Is Superdesk suitable for small newsrooms?
faqs.answer: Yes, Sourcefabric offers Superdesk Lite for organizations with up to 25 users, alongside the option to self-host the free open-source version at any scale.

faqs.question: What kind of content can Superdesk publish to?
faqs.answer: Superdesk can publish to web, print, mobile, and social media, and can build stories from wire services, RSS, and social sources.

use_cases.title: Newsroom editorial workflow
use_cases.description: News organizations manage story creation, review, and publishing in one system.

use_cases.title: Multi-channel news distribution
use_cases.description: Publishers distribute the same stories across web, print, mobile, and social.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Superdesk is an open-source, headless newsroom CMS from Sourcefabric for news creation, editorial workflow, and multi-channel publishing.

enrichment.short_description: Textpattern is a free, open-source PHP content management system known for its tag-based templating and lean codebase.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Textpattern originated in 2001 when Dean Allen built it to publish his personal site, and was formally announced as a CMS in 2003. It's free, open-source software licensed under the GNU General Public License v2.0, with development ongoing on GitHub.
The CMS is known for its tag-based template language, built-in Textile text-formatting support (plus Markdown via plugin), and a lean codebase aimed at fast page loads.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Textpattern suits writers, bloggers, and developers who want a lightweight, uncluttered CMS with a flexible template language rather than a heavier, plugin-dependent platform.

features.title: Tag-based template language
features.description: A dedicated templating syntax for structuring pages and manipulating content.

features.title: Textile and Markdown formatting
features.description: Built-in Textile support, with Markdown available via plugin.

features.title: Plugin extensibility
features.description: Hundreds of community-built plugins extend core functionality.

features.title: Lean, fast codebase
features.description: Minimal core designed for fast loading and efficient resource use.

features.title: Uncluttered admin interface
features.description: Administration panels designed to avoid unnecessary clutter and remain accessible.

pros: Free and open source under GPL v2
Lightweight, fast-loading codebase
Flexible tag-based templating for custom site structures
Long-running project with over two decades of development
cons: Smaller plugin and theme ecosystem than WordPress
Tag-based template syntax has a learning curve for newcomers
Less suited to complex, large-scale sites than heavier CMS platforms

faqs.question: Is Textpattern free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Textpattern is completely free, open-source software under the GNU General Public License v2.0.

faqs.question: When was Textpattern created?
faqs.answer: Dean Allen originally built it in 2001, and it was formally announced as Textpattern in 2003.

faqs.question: What text formatting does Textpattern support?
faqs.answer: Textpattern ships with built-in Textile support, and Markdown is available via plugin.

faqs.question: Is Textpattern actively developed?
faqs.answer: Yes, development continues on GitHub under the Textpattern project.

use_cases.title: Personal and professional blogging
use_cases.description: Writers publish blogs using Textpattern's lightweight editing and formatting tools.

use_cases.title: Small to mid-size websites
use_cases.description: Developers build lean, fast-loading sites using Textpattern's tag-based templates.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Textpattern is a free, open-source PHP CMS with a tag-based template language, built-in Textile support, and a lean codebase, since 2001.
